title: Friendship & Cooperation
objective: Students will explore friendship & cooperation concepts including Self-Reflection in Relationships, Giving and Receiving Feedback, Assertive Communication and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Self-Reflection in Relationships? Let's find out together!

Reflect on their own role and behaviour in relationships — recognising patterns in how they interact with others, understanding what they contribute to friendships, and identifying areas where they could improve as a friend or team member
Let's practice together! Identify a pattern in their own social behaviour, such as always wanting to lead or avoiding confrontation
Can you honestly reflect on their friendships and say something like 'I think I sometimes take over in group work and I'm trying to listen more' — showing awareness of their own social patterns?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Self-Reflection in Relationships: Identify a pattern in their own social behaviour, such as always wanting to lead or avoiding confrontation Describe what they do well in relationships and one area they'd like to improve
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Giving and Receiving Feedback!

Give and receive constructive feedback — telling someone what they did well and what could be improved in a way that is helpful rather than hurtful, and receiving feedback about their own work without becoming defensive
Let me show you something cool. Give feedback that includes both a specific positive point and a constructive suggestion
If you reviews a classmate's story and finds mistakes, can they give feedback that's helpful — like 'I loved this part, and this bit could be clearer' — rather than just saying 'it's wrong'?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Giving and Receiving Feedback. Give feedback that includes both a specific positive point and a constructive suggestion Receive feedback about their own work without getting upset or dismissive
Let's discover the amazing world of Assertive Communication!

Use assertive communication — expressing needs, opinions, and boundaries clearly and respectfully without being aggressive (pushy/demanding) or passive (giving in/staying silent) — including saying no when something doesn't feel right
Time to try this out: Explain the difference between assertive, aggressive, and passive responses
If friends want you to do something they're uncomfortable with — like excluding another child or breaking a rule — can you say 'I don't want to do that' firmly but politely?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Assertive Communication: Explain the difference between assertive, aggressive, and passive responses Role-play saying no in a peer pressure scenario
Have you ever thought about Helping Others Resolve Conflicts? Let's find out together!
Mediate conflicts between others — helping two friends who are arguing by listening to both sides, helping them see each other's perspective, and guiding them toward a fair resolution
Let's practice together! Listen to both sides of a conflict without taking sides initially
If two of you's friends are arguing and both come to you for support, can you help them talk it out fairly rather than just taking one side?
Great job! Today we learned about Helping Others Resolve Conflicts. Listen to both sides of a conflict without taking sides initially Help each person articulate their perspective to the other
